common_name: Lady of the Night (Night Jasmine, Brunfelsia americana)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in partial to full shade in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil; Lady of the Night thrives in the dappled shade of large trees and performs poorly in full afternoon sun in zone 9. In zone 9a, it requires frost protection or container culture that can be brought indoors; in zone 9b-10, it grows as a reliable evergreen shrub. The fragrance is most intense after dark — plant near outdoor seating areas for evening enjoyment. All parts are toxic.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; Lady of the Night is one of the finest shade-tolerant fragrant shrubs for zone 9-11 Gulf Coast gardens, where its powerful nocturnal fragrance fills outdoor spaces on warm spring and summer evenings. Brunfelsia americana is an evergreen shrub from the Caribbean (Jamaica, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Lesser Antilles) in the Solanaceae family closely related to Yesterday Today Tomorrow (B. pauciflora and B. australis). Its single-day flowers begin as pure white tubes and age through cream to pale yellow — the reverse color change from B. pauciflora's purple-to-white progression. The flower fragrance is released most powerfully after dark and is one of the most intensely sweet and complex fragrances in the plant world — often described as combining jasmine, vanilla, and gardenias — with a reach that can fill a large garden or patio on warm, still evenings. In zone 9, Lady of the Night blooms from spring through fall with peak flowering in late spring and early summer. The combination of shade tolerance and extraordinary nocturnal fragrance makes it one of the most valuable plants for shaded evening garden spaces, planted near patios, decks, and seating areas. CAUTION: All parts of the plant are toxic — contain brunfelsamidine and other alkaloids causing neurological symptoms if ingested.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Brunfelsia americana (Lady of the Night)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Brunfelsia americana
LSU AgCenter — Fragrant Shade Plants for Gulf Coast Louisiana
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Brunfelsia species (toxic to pets)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded container plant in zone 9; Philodendron gloriosum cannot tolerate temperatures below 60°F. Unlike most philodendrons that climb, P. gloriosum is a terrestrial crawler that spreads horizontally on its thick rhizome — plant in a wide, shallow container to accommodate this growth habit. Use a well-aerated potting mix of orchid bark, perlite, and potting soil. Water thoroughly, then allow to slightly dry between waterings. All parts toxic if chewed.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Glorious Philodendron needs consistent bright indirect light for healthy growth and the maintenance of its velvety leaf texture, but will burn in direct sun. Philodendron gloriosum is a terrestrial (ground-crawling, not climbing) philodendron native to the humid tropical forests of Colombia, where it creeps along the forest floor on a thick, horizontal rhizome. Unlike the majority of popular philodendrons that climb by aerial roots, P. gloriosum spreads sideways — each new leaf is produced from a stem node extending along the surface of the growing medium, giving the plant a distinctly different and dramatic growth habit. The leaves are enormous when mature (up to 24–36 inches on well-grown specimens), heart-shaped, with a velvety, deep emerald-green surface crossed by bold white veins in a pattern that inspired the specific epithet 'gloriosum.' The velvety texture results from tiny papillae on the leaf surface that scatter light, creating the characteristic soft, matte sheen. Glorious Philodendron has become one of the most sought-after collector aroids internationally, valued for both its spectacular foliage and its unusual growth habit. All parts are mildly toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron gloriosum species profile
American Society for Horticultural Science — Philodendron gloriosum ornamental notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron species for indoor tropical culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron species (toxic to pets)

common_name: White Mussaenda (Philippine Mussaenda, White Flag Bush, Donna Luz)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in late spring in full sun to partial shade with well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; White Mussaenda grows rapidly in zone 9-11 and blooms most heavily from late spring through fall when temperatures are warm. In zone 9a, it is frost-tender and must be protected — use as a large container specimen brought indoors in winter. In zone 9b-10, it can be grown outdoors as a large shrub. The Donna Luz and related hybrid forms produce vastly enlarged, more numerous white bracts than the original species.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; White Mussaenda grows most vigorously and blooms most heavily in full sun but tolerates and benefits from afternoon shade in zone 9's intense summer heat. Mussaenda philippica is a large tropical shrub from the Philippines in the coffee family (Rubiaceae) that has produced some of the most spectacular ornamental hybrids in tropical horticulture — particularly through the Philippine breeding program that developed the 'Dona' series (Dona Aurora, Dona Carmen, Dona Luz, Dona Esperanza) named in honor of Philippine First Ladies, in which the small, typically 5-petaled flowers are surrounded by greatly enlarged, pure white, snowflake-shaped sepals (not petals) that cover the entire branch tips in a cloud of brilliant white. The ornamental display comes entirely from these enlarged sepals, which function to attract pollinators to the small true flowers within — a strategy shared with bougainvillea and poinsettia. The Dona Luz cultivar has become one of the most widely planted tropical ornamentals in zone 10-11 landscapes worldwide for its exceptionally long bloom season (essentially year-round in zone 10-11), clean white appearance, and dramatic presence as a large specimen shrub.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Mussaenda philippica (White Mussaenda)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Mussaenda philippica
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Mussaenda cultivation notes
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Ornamentals for Gulf Coast Zone 10 Landscapes

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring after last frost in full sun with well-drained soil; Purple Allamanda is a vigorous grower that can be trained as a large sprawling shrub or tied to a trellis as a vine. In zone 9a, frost damage is likely in winter — grow in a large container or plant in a protected microclimate. In zone 9b-10, it grows as a perennial shrub-vine and blooms from late spring through fall. The milky sap is toxic and a skin and eye irritant.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Purple Allamanda requires full sun for best flowering and will produce fewer blooms in shaded conditions. Allamanda blanchetii (sometimes listed as A. violacea) is a large, vigorous, scrambling shrub or lax vine from Brazil, closely related to the common Golden Allamanda (A. cathartica) but producing rich rose-purple to magenta, trumpet-shaped flowers 3–4 inches in diameter rather than the familiar yellow. The flowers are stunning — the color a deep, saturated rose-magenta that is unusual among tropical vines — and are borne in successive flushes from late spring through frost in zone 9. Like its golden relative, Purple Allamanda has a sprawling, lax habit that can be trained on a fence or trellis by tying stems, or allowed to cascade down a bank or over a wall. The stems exude a milky white sap when cut, similar to the toxic latex of its relatives in the Apocynaceae family — this sap is a skin irritant and toxic if ingested, with all plant parts considered poisonous. In zone 9, Purple Allamanda is used as a warm-season annual or half-hardy perennial; in zone 10-11 it grows as a large, floriferous evergreen shrub that blooms nearly year-round.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Allamanda blanchetii (Purple Allamanda)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Allamanda blanchetii

ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Allamanda (toxic to pets)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines after last frost in full sun to partial shade on a trellis, obelisk, or fence; White Mandevilla grows rapidly in warm conditions and can be kept in a container for portability. In zone 9, treat as a warm-season annual or bring containers indoors before frost. The species is slightly more cold-sensitive than red-flowered Mandevilla hybrids. Train the twining stems onto supports early — stems can become tangled if left to sprawl.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; White Mandevilla blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates morning sun with afternoon shade in zone 9, where this combination can extend the flowering season through the hottest months. Mandevilla boliviensis is a twining tropical vine from Bolivia and Ecuador that produces pure white, 3-inch, funnel-shaped flowers with a contrasting yellow center — a clean, elegant color combination that pairs beautifully with other tropical colors or with purely white garden designs. The flowers are produced profusely and continuously throughout the warm months on vigorous, evergreen stems clothed in glossy, dark-green, lance-shaped leaves. White Mandevilla is the primary white species used in breeding the modern 'White' selections of Mandevilla sold in mass-market retail — though most commercial 'white' Mandevillas are actually complex hybrids combining M. boliviensis with other species for improved plant size and flower production. Like all Mandevilla relatives (family Apocynaceae), the plant produces a milky latex sap when cut that is irritating to skin and eyes. In zone 9, it is one of the finest annual or temporary perennial vines for rapid seasonal color on trellises and fences, growing 6–10 feet in a single season.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Mandevilla species for Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Mandevilla for Gulf Coast container and landscape use
